task.title: Configuring FreeRADIUS to authenticate network clients securely by using EAP

li.p: You installed the freeradius and freeradius-ldap packages.
The configuration files in the /etc/raddb/ directory are unchanged and as provided by the freeradius packages.
The host is enrolled in a Red Hat Enterprise Linux Identity Management (IdM) domain.

context.p: FreeRADIUS supports different methods of the Extensible authentication protocol (EAP). However, for a supported and secure scenario, use EAP-TTLS (tunneled transport layer security).

With EAP-TTLS, the clients use a secure TLS connection as the outer authentication protocol to set up the tunnel. The inner authentication then uses LDAP to authenticate to Identity Management. To use EAP-TTLS, you need a TLS server certificate.

note.p: The default FreeRADIUS configuration files serve as documentation and describe all parameters and directives. If you want to disable certain features, comment them out instead of removing the corresponding parts in the configuration files. This enables you to preserve the structure of the configuration files and the included documentation.

step.cmd: Create a private key and request a certificate from IdM:

info.codeblock: # ipa-getcert request -w -k /etc/pki/tls/private/radius.key -f /etc/pki/tls/certs/radius.pem -o "root:radiusd" -m 640 -O "root:radiusd" -M 640 -T caIPAserviceCert -C 'systemctl restart radiusd.service' -N freeradius.idm.example.com -D freeradius.idm.example.com -K radius/freeradius.idm.example.com
info.p: The certmonger service stores the private key in the /etc/pki/tls/private/radius.key file and the certificate in the /etc/pki/tls/certs/radius.pem file, and it sets secure permissions. Additionally, certmonger will monitor the certificate, renew it before it expires, and restart the radiusd service after the certificate was renewed.

step.cmd: Verify that the CA successfully issued the certificate:

info.codeblock: # ipa-getcert list -f /etc/pki/tls/certs/radius.pem ... Number of certificates and requests being tracked: 1. Request ID '20240918142211': status: MONITORING stuck: no key pair storage: type=FILE,location='/etc/pki/tls/private/radius.key' certificate: type=FILE,location='/etc/pki/tls/certs/radius.crt' ...

step.cmd: Create the /etc/raddb/certs/dh file with Diffie-Hellman (DH) parameters. For example, to create a DH file with a 2048 bits prime, enter:

info.p: For security reasons, do not use a DH file with less than a 2048 bits prime. Depending on the number of bits, the creation of the file can take several minutes.

li.p: Configure the TLS-related settings in the tls-config tls-common directive:
Set the default_eap_type parameter in the eap directive to ttls:
Comment out the md5 directives to disable the insecure EAP-MD5 authentication method:
Note that, in the default configuration file, other insecure EAP authentication methods are commented out by default.

step.cmd: Edit the /etc/raddb/sites-available/default file, and comment out all authentication methods other than eap:

info.p: This leaves only EAP enabled for the outer authentication and disables plain-text authentication methods.

step.cmd: Edit the /etc/raddb/sites-available/inner-tunnel file, and make the following changes:

li.p: Comment out the -ldap entry and add the ldap module configuration to the authorize directive:
Uncomment the LDAP authentication type in the authenticate directive:

step.cmd: Edit the /etc/raddb/mods-available/ldap file, and make the following changes:

li.p: In the ldap directive, set the IdM LDAP server URL and the base distinguished name (DN):
Specify the ldaps protocol in the server URL to use TLS-encrypted connections between the FreeRADIUS host and the IdM server.
In the ldap directive, enable TLS certificate validation of the IdM LDAP server:
Set a secure password in the localhost and localhost_ipv6 client directives:
Add a client directive for the network authenticator:
Optional: If other hosts should also be able to access the FreeRADIUS service, add client directives for them as well, for example:

The ipaddr parameter accepts IPv4 and IPv6 addresses, and you can use the optional classless inter-domain routing (CIDR) notation to specify ranges. However, you can set only one value in this parameter. For example, to grant access to both an IPv4 and IPv6 address, you must add two client directives.
Use a descriptive name for the client directive, such as a hostname or a word that describes where the IP range is used.

info.codeblock: # radiusd -XC ... Configuration appears to be OK

step.cmd: Open the RADIUS ports in the firewalld service:
Enable and start the radiusd service:

p.xref: Testing EAP-TTLS authentication against a FreeRADIUS server or authenticator

li.p: If the radiusd service fails to start, verify that you can resolve the IdM server host name:
For other problems, run radiusd in debug mode:
Stop the radiusd service:
Start the service in debug mode:
Perform authentication tests on the FreeRADIUS host, as referenced in the Verification section.
Disable no longer required authentication methods and other features you do not use.
